data.title: AI Demand Could Crowd Out TSMC's Capacity — Market Talk
data.description:
0255 GMT - Robust AI demand could crowd out TSMC's capacity, posing risks to its market share, Deutsche Bank's Robert Sanders says in a research note. As TSMC tries to narrow the gap between supply and demand of the leading-edge chips, some customers could worry about the excessively tight foundry capacity and a potential shortage, the analyst says. Together with TSMC's significant price rises proposed for 2026, it could offer up share opportunities to competitors, most notably Samsung, despite its execution issues, the analyst notes. Qualcomm might consider Samsung for producing 2-nanometer chips, according to media reports. "What would be more concerning is if Samsung was able to get more meaningful share outside of Qualcomm, such as at Nvidia," the analyst adds. (sherry.qin@wsj.com) 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  October 16, 2025 23:10 ET (03:10 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: My father died leaving an old will predating his fourth wife. Who inherits his estate?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'He gave his fourth wife power of attorney and access to his bank account to pay bills'

"A will from 2019 mentions that he's a single man and leaves his residual estate to six children."(Photo subjects are models.)

Dear Quentin,

My father passed away. We tried over the years to have him fill us in on his estate plans and final wishes. He said we would be notified. He prepaid for most of his memorial service, although there was still about $2,000 we had to pay for.

Here is some background on his will, beneficiaries and wives.

His third wife passed away seven years ago. A will from 2019 mentions that he's a single man and leaves his residual estate to six children. He married his fourth wife in 2022. He was the primary caregiver for her, as she has health issues. His will was not updated. In 2024, he had a stroke. They sold their home, vehicles and most of their possessions in 2025.

For various reasons they resided in different long-term care facilities. He gave his fourth wife power of attorney and access to his bank account to pay bills. She's been moving money from his account. Towards the end they had a falling out, and she doesn't want to be involved in his memorial service. Our relationship with her is cordial at best.

In picking up his possessions, we discovered the 2019 will, which made one of his children his "personal representative." There is also a life-insurance policy making his third wife the primary beneficiary and his natural children secondary beneficiaries. We're concerned about our responsibilities, particularly his taxes.

At this point we don't believe there is any cash in his account to pay any potential taxes. Any words of wisdom are appreciated.

One of His Children

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com, and follow Quentin Fottrell on X, the platform formerly known as Twitter.

Don't miss: 'Today is my 61st birthday': I have my ex-spouse's Social Security benefits. Should I retire at 65 and travel?

Beneficiaries trump a last will and testament.

Dear One,

This is the order of merit for your father's estate: (1) primary beneficiaries, (2) secondary beneficiaries and (3) those mentioned in his 2019 will and his fourth wife.

Life insurance: Beneficiaries trump a last will and testament. Any living beneficiary will inherit money from a life-insurance policy, because the contract exists between the company and the beneficiary. I presume the secondary beneficiaries named by your father will inherit the proceeds of his policy, given that his third wife died before he did. I sincerely doubt the money would go to his third wife's heirs in this case. If the wording is not clear, a probate attorney would be able to sort it out.

Estate taxes: Any estate taxes or overdue taxes would come out of your father's estate, and it is his current wife's responsibility, not yours, to settle his estate. But there seems to be confusion over whether your father added his fourth wife to his bank account as a co-signer - which would mean she does not own the money in the account - or as a co-owner. Call your father's bank to inform them of his death, and get a copy of the death certificate by contacting the vital records office in the state or county where he died. Death certificates are considered public records.

The old will: This is messy. Your father's old will is still technically valid, despite his remarriage. However, most states have "omitted spouse" or "pretermitted spouse" statutes to protect the spouse of a person who did not update their will after marrying. These laws vary by state, but it's likely that your father's fourth wife would be entitled to a share of his estate, which could be one-third or as much as half of the remaining estate. This would include proceeds from the sale of your father's house and other possessions.

An 'omitted' spouse: Using California as an example, if your father's fourth wife is not mentioned in the will, she is entitled to receive a share of the estate equivalent to what she would have received if your father had died intestate, or without a will, according to the Law Offices of Mark Ruiz, located in the Bay Area. "The law ensures that the pretermitted heir is treated fairly and receives a portion of the estate as if there was no will in place," the firm says. If she received other assets or if the omission was intentional, that could change things.

Appointing an executor: Your father's death renders your stepmother's power of attorney void. If he did not appoint an administrator or executor of his estate, the probate court will appoint one, typically giving priority to a living spouse. Alternatively, you could petition the probate court as an interested party (a beneficiary or heir) to be the administrator/executor. If your father and his wife co-owned the home they sold, those funds would be deemed community or marital property and, as such, would be out of the reach of your father's children.

Debtors making claims: "If you owe taxes when you die, the IRS will attempt to collect the tax debt from your estate," says Atlanta, Ga.-based Wiggam Law. "In cases where there isn't an estate, the IRS generally won't be able to collect the tax bill. You must file a final tax return if you would have been obligated to file a tax return if you were alive. Your surviving spouse or the representative of your estate can file the return. If you die and your only income for that year was Social Security, no one needs to file a final return for you."

And if there is no money left in the estate after your father's taxes and other costs have been settled, your problems are effectively solved.

Related: 'How do I shield my retirement savings?' I'm worried about Trump's trade war and Fed's willingness to cut interest rates

The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

My husband and I are in our 50s - and 85% invested in stocks. Is our strategy too aggressive for the next 10 years?

Can my husband contest his late brother's $600K will? He experienced oxygen deprivation due to COPD before he died.

I have early Alzheimer's and my husband has stage 4 kidney disease. We just inherited $50K. How can this help us?

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where we look for answers to life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  10-16-25 2300ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Spotify says it's working to protect artists from AI abuse, but the streaming company's track record is shaky
data.description:
By Lukas I. Alpert

After years of fighting scams and fraud, the music-streaming company announced it is joining forces with three major music labels to develop AI products that can help artists

Spotify says it works hard to protect artists and their ability to make money, but many have criticized the service for paying most working musicians a pittance. "Spotify is probably the worst thing that has happened to musicians," Icelandic singer Bjork has said.

It was arguably one of the most egregious examples of artificial intelligence being used to take advantage of a music-streaming platform.

Federal prosecutors said last year that a fraudster had bilked numerous music-streaming services out of at least $10 million in royalties by using bots to listen to thousands of songs he had generated through AI programs.

It was just one instance that led many artists to view AI as simply another thing that can eat away at their ability to make a living in what is already a tough business. The alleged scam, and others like it, led to extensive soul-searching by streaming services on how to better protect artists from people abusing AI to siphon off royalty payments.

On Thursday, Spotify Technology S.A. (SPOT) announced that it is partnering with major record labels Sony Music Group (SONY), Universal Music Group (NL:UMG) and Warner Music Group (WMG), as well as digital-licensing company Merlin and distributor Believe, to develop AI products that can help artists.

"If the music industry doesn't lead in this moment, AI-powered innovation will happen elsewhere, without rights, consent, or compensation," Spotify said in announcing the launch of a research team that will work to better connect artists with fans and weed out bad actors.

Neither Spotify nor the record labels involved offered much detail as to what the new products might look like.

Spotify has long been criticized by musicians, songwriters and artists' rights groups, who have accused the company of paying a pittance to most of the working artists whose songs appear on its platform.

Earlier this year, Spotify said it paid $10 billion in royalties in 2024, but only about 4% of the 225,000 artists whose work appears on the platform made what would amount a comfortable living.

Fraud involving streaming platforms is not new, but it has become hypercharged by AI technology, which has helped scammers quickly generate scores of songs that can more readily evade detection.

In another case earlier this year, Spotify took down a new, unauthorized AI-generated song published under the name of outlaw-country singer Blaze Foley, who died in 1989.

The problem runs deep. The International Confederation of Societies of Authors and Composers has estimated that AI-generated music could potentially draw nearly $12 billion in revenue away from living, breathing artists over the next five years alone.

Not all of this is necessarily due to criminal fraud. Earlier this year, an AI-generated band called the Velvet Sundown was able to attract millions of streams of its 1970s-inflected songs on Spotify.

Spotify has said that it doesn't intend to ban outright the use of AI in generating music that appears on its platform, but that it is working to identify fraud and other violations of its user agreement. In September, the company said it was tightening up its spam filters as well as the rules around impersonation and for clearly identifying songs made by AI.

"At its best, AI is unlocking incredible new ways for artists to create music and for listeners to discover it. At its worst, AI can be used by bad actors and content farms to confuse or deceive listeners, push 'slop' into the ecosystem, and interfere with authentic artists working to build their careers," the company said.

-Lukas I. Alpert

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  10-16-25 2223ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Starbucks Evaluating Offers for China Business
data.description:
By Megan Cheah 




  Starbucks is assessing bids from five parties for its China business as it seeks a partner to navigate the country's competitive coffee landscape. 



  "We've had very strong interest from multiple, high-quality partners all of whom share our confidence in the long-term growth potential of Starbucks in China," said a Starbucks spokesperson. 



  The spokesperson said that five bidders had provided bids, and the company was in the process of evaluating them. 



  The company estimates the value of the deal to be over $10 billion, including royalties, the upfront investment by a potential partner, and Starbucks's retained stake in the China business. 



  The news was first reported by the Financial Times. 



  The Seattle-based beverages giant is facing intense competition in China, one of its main markets, with local chains such as Luckin Coffee opening more stores, offering cheaper drinks and easy-to-use mobile apps. 



  At the same time, more U.S.-based companies have been seeking to exit or pare their exposure to China as trade tensions rise between the world's two largest economies. 





  Write to Megan Cheah at megan.cheah@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  October 16, 2025 22:09 ET (02:09 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Bank First Corporation Receives Regulatory Approval to Acquire Centre 1 Bancorp, Inc.
data.description:
Bank First Corporation Receives Regulatory Approval to Acquire Centre 1 Bancorp, Inc.

Bank First Corporation Receives Regulatory Approval to Acquire Centre 1 Bancorp, Inc.

PR Newswire

MANITOWOC, Wis,, Oct. 16, 2025

MANITOWOC, Wis,, Oct. 16, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- Bank First Corporation (Nasdaq: BFC) ("Bank First"), the holding company of Bank First, N.A., announced today that it has received all required regulatory approvals to complete its planned merger with Centre 1 Bancorp, Inc. ("Centre"), parent company of The First National Bank and Trust ("First National Bank and Trust"). The transaction will close on January 1, 2026, subject to customary closing conditions and approval by Centre's shareholders.

"Receiving regulatory approval allows us to begin the transition toward operating as Bank First, a move that reflects our shared commitment to long-term growth and community-focused banking," said Mike Molepske, Chairman and Chief Executive Officer of Bank First. "This merger brings together two institutions with aligned values and complementary strengths, positioning us to better serve our customers with expanded resources, broader capabilities, and a continued focus on relationship banking."

Following the close of the transaction, First National Bank and Trust will become a division of Bank First until the system conversion, expected to take place in the second quarter of 2026. At that time, all First National Bank and Trust offices will fully transition to the Bank First name, and customers will be transitioned to the Bank First system and digital banking platform.

Bank First Corporation and Bank First, N.A.
Bank First Corporation is the holding company for Bank First, N.A., a relationship-focused financial institution headquartered in Manitowoc, Wisconsin. With total assets of approximately $4.4 billion, the bank offers a comprehensive range of financial services, including commercial, mortgage, and consumer lending, as well as various deposit and savings options. It primarily serves customers throughout central and northeastern Wisconsin. Founded in 1894, the institution has a long-standing history of supporting the communities it serves. For more information, visit www.bankfirst.com.

About Centre 1 Bancorp, Inc. and The First National Bank and Trust Company
Centre 1 Bancorp, Inc. is the holding company of The First National Bank and Trust Company, a family-owned community bank headquartered in Beloit, Wisconsin. Chartered in 1882, First National Bank and Trust Company serves customers across southern Wisconsin and northern Illinois with a strong commitment to full-service banking, investments, asset management, and exceptional service. Its dedication to delivering Sound Advice has helped position the bank as a trusted leader in the Stateline region. For additional information, visit www.bankatfirstnational.com.

Contacts
 Bank First:  Mike Molepske, Chairman & CEO, at mmolepske@bankfirst.com or (920) 652-3202
Centre:   Don O'Day, President, at don.oday@bankatfirstnational.com or (608) 363-8007

For further information, contact:
Deb Weyker, SVP Marketing
Phone: (920) 652-3274, dweyker@bankfirst.com

View original content:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/bank-first-corporation-receives-regulatory-approval-to-acquire-centre-1-bancorp-inc-302587084.html

SOURCE Bank First Corporation

data.title: CORRECTING and REPLACING Former PayPal and Former Intuit CEO Bill Harris to Showcase Evergreen Wealth and Tax Strategies for Options, RSUs and QSBS
data.description:
CORRECTING and REPLACING Former PayPal and Former Intuit CEO Bill Harris to Showcase Evergreen Wealth and Tax Strategies for Options, RSUs and QSBS

Serial fintech pioneer Harris will share how founders, funders and senior executives who generate large equity value can take advantage of multiple advanced tax strategies to reduce or, in some cases, eliminate taxes on their company equity

Please replace the release dated October 15, 2025, with the following corrected version due to multiple revisions.

This press release features multimedia. View the full release here: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20251015135420/en/

Serial fintech entrepreneur Bill Harris, founder and CEO of Evergreen Wealth, a digital technology-driven, next-generation RIA, former CEO of PayPal, former CEO of Intuit, and founding CEO of Personal Capital, a wealth management firm he started and grew to $23 billion in assets

The updated release reads:

FORMER PAYPAL AND FORMER INTUIT CEO BILL HARRIS TO SHOWCASE EVERGREEN WEALTH AND TAX STRATEGIES FOR OPTIONS, RSUS AND QSBS

Serial fintech pioneer Harris will share how founders, funders and senior executives who generate large equity value can take advantage of multiple advanced tax strategies to reduce or, in some cases, eliminate taxes on their company equity

Evergreen Wealth:

WHAT: “Save the Date” to join serial fintech entrepreneur, Bill Harris, former CEO of PayPal, former CEO of Intuit, and founder of Personal Capital, to discuss his latest venture, Evergreen Wealth, a digital, next-generation Registered Investment Advisor (RIA) that provides tax-aware investment management and AI-powered financial advice to affluent and high-net-worth clients seeking to build long-term wealth. The company recently launched with more than $100 million in assets under management.

Evergreen Wealth is built on the learnings from Harris’s previous ventures and leadership — PayPal (financial technology innovation), Intuit and TurboTax (taxes) and Personal Capital (investment management) — to create a next-generation wealth advisory firm that integrates human guidance with intelligent technology that is designed to deliver higher after-tax performance for high-net-worth individuals and their families.

WHO: Bill Harris, founder and CEO of Evergreen Wealth, a digital technology-driven, next-generation RIA, former CEO of PayPal, former CEO of Intuit, and founding CEO of Personal Capital, a wealth management firm he started and grew to $23 billion in assets. Harris will be joined by Christopher Karachale, a leading QSBS (Qualified Small Business Stock) attorney in Silicon Valley and Partner at Hanson Bridgett. Harris has personally worked with Karachale on multiple exits and will share how proper tax planning can reduce, or in some cases, eliminate taxes when selling company stock.

WHY: Founders, Funders and senior executives who generate considerable equity value also generate large tax liabilities. There are multiple advanced tax strategies that can reduce or, in some cases, even eliminate tax on the company stock that high-net-worth executives receive, including:

  • ISOs, Non-Quals and RSUs
  • Qualified Small Business Stock (QSBS)
  • Concentrated positions and tax transitions
  • Charitable and estate considerations

WHEN and WHERE:

  • Monday, October 20, from 4:00 - 5:30 pm at The Battery in San Francisco
  • Wednesday, October 22, from 4:00 - 5:30 pm at the Rosewood Sand Hill in Menlo Park

PRESS RSVP & INTERVIEWS: Accredited press can schedule an interview with Harris and Karachale in San Francisco on Monday, October 20, at The Battery, or in Menlo Park on Wednesday, October 22, at the Rosewood Sand Hill. Harris is also available for one-on-one scheduled interviews with the media on Tuesday, October 21. Accredited media are kindly requested to RSVP, register their interest or request an interview at evergreenwealth@wearemgp.com.

FOUNDER, FUNDER & SENIOR EXECUTIVE RSVP: Silicon Valley founders, funders and senior executives with a minimum of $250,000 in investable assets are encouraged to request an invitation to Evergreen Wealth’s Silicon Valley events at either The Battery or the Rosewood Sand Hill in Menlo Park. Attendees will receive a complimentary iPad as a thank you for attending the event.* Please email evergreenwealth@wearemgp.com to request a personal invitation from Bill Harris. Please note that space is limited and subject to pre-approval and availability.

*Invited attendees will receive a complimentary iPad (approximate value: $350) upon participating in the event. Limit one per attendee. There is no obligation to open an account or establish a relationship with Evergreen Wealth.

About Evergreen Wealth

Evergreen Wealth is a Registered Investment Advisor (RIA) registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) that provides investment management and financial advice to affluent and high-net-worth individuals and families. We build custom-engineered, tax-optimized Dynamic Portfolios for our clients, purposefully designed to deliver higher after-tax performance, and financial advice through the combination of investment advisors and Evergreen Intelligence, an agentic AI advice engine.

Founded by fintech pioneer Bill Harris, the Evergreen Wealth team has offices in Miami, Dallas and Raleigh, NC. Follow Evergreen Wealth on LinkedIn or visit evergreenwealth.com to learn more.

Media Contacts:
Mindy M. Hull
Evergreen Wealth PR
+1 415 889 9977
evergreenwealth@wearemgp.com

Abbie Sheridan
Evergreen Wealth PR
+1 516 286 7056
evergreenwealth@wearemgp.com

data.title: Kaimore's VITA Program Returns Nearly One Million Dollars to Working Families
data.description:
Kaimore's VITA Program Returns Nearly One Million Dollars to Working Families

Kaimore's VITA Program Returns Nearly One Million Dollars to Working Families

PR Newswire

LOS ANGELES, Oct. 16, 2025

Free tax assistance transforms lives through financial empowerment and community care

LOS ANGELES, Oct. 16, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- This tax season, Kaimore's Volunteer Income Tax Assistance (VITA) program helped over 500 households file their taxes — at no cost — bringing back nearly 1 million dollars in refunds and tax credits to local families. For many low- and moderate-income households, each appointment was more than a transaction — it was a lifeline. For a single mother, it meant finally catching up on rent. For a retired couple, it meant breathing room after months of medical bills. For parents of young children, it meant accessing life-changing credits that covered school supplies, groceries, and a little savings for the future.

Through the program, families collectively received over $700,000 in federal refunds and $230,000 in state refunds. These funds help cover essential living expenses that can make or break a family's stability: rent and utility payments, medical costs, childcare, and transportation. For households living paycheck to paycheck, receiving a few thousand dollars back often means avoiding eviction, paying down debt, or finally being able to start saving.

The impact extends beyond the refunds themselves. Kaimore helped families access $334,503 in Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) — one of the nation's most effective anti-poverty tools — and an additional $229,633 through the Child Tax Credit (CTC) and Additional CTC combined. These credits directly support working parents, reducing child poverty and ensuring more children grow up in financially stable homes.

"These numbers represent real lives changed," says Joyce Lam, Executive Director. "When a single parent receives an Earned Income Tax Credit refund, it might mean catching up on bills or buying groceries without fear. When a family receives a Child Tax Credit, it means they can finally afford school clothes or set aside a little for the future. This is dignity restored through access and education."

Kaimore's VITA program is part of the Financial Literacy portion of their signature Market Ready program and offers free, IRS-certified tax preparation for low- to moderate-income individuals and families. Without VITA, many would rely on costly commercial tax services that can charge hundreds of dollars — cutting into the refunds families depend on.

"Time and time again we meet clients who pay $200 or more to get their taxes done even though they have a simple tax return. This program helps them get their taxes filed for free which takes a burden from them," says Ariana Garcia, VITA Site Coordinator. By removing that barrier, Kaimore ensures that every eligible taxpayer receives the full credits and refunds they have earned.

The benefits ripple outward into the broader community. The dollars returned through VITA are spent locally — at grocery stores, auto repair shops, childcare centers, and small businesses — helping to stimulate the neighborhood economy and keep resources circulating where they are most needed.

This year's program was made possible with support from Toberman Neighborhood Center, East West Bank, UCLA's Bruins Assistance for All (BAFA) club, and local Enrolled Agents and volunteers, whose time, funding, and expertise made this community-wide impact possible.

Kaimore's VITA program will continue offering free, IRS-certified tax preparation services, ensuring that every eligible household can access the credits and refunds they deserve. Kaimore is now seeking new sponsors and partners to help expand this vital program in the next tax season, with an emphasis on reaching families, single mothers, veterans, and transition-aged youth who are often underserved in traditional financial systems.

"At Kaimore, we're not just helping people file taxes — we're helping them rebuild their confidence and their future. Each partnership allows us to reach more people who might otherwise slip through the cracks," says Lam. "By helping people access what they've already earned, we help them reclaim control of their futures."

For more information on the VITA program or to become a sponsor, visit www.kaimore.org or email info@kaimore.org.

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/kaimores-vita-program-returns-nearly-one-million-dollars-to-working-families-302587039.html

SOURCE Kaimore

data.title: Lithium Americas is deemed vital by Trump, but investors beware, J.P. Morgan says
data.description:
By James Rogers

Shares of Lithium Americas tumbled on J.P. Morgan's downgrade

The federal government recently took a stake in lithium producer Lithium Americas Corp.

Lithium Americas Corp. saw its stock surge recently after the federal government took a stake in the company, but J.P. Morgan warns that investors should not get carried away with the the lithium producer's newfound popularity in Washington.

The lithium industry, like those for rare earths and chips, has been deemed vital for national security by the Trump administration. The element is an important component in rechargeable batteries that power electric and hybrid vehicles, as well as a variety of consumer electronics. Fueled by the Trump administration's support, Lithium Americas' stock (LAC) is up 129.5% over the past month.

But J.P. Morgan sees the company's valuation as expensive and downgraded the stock to underweight from neutral Wednesday. "We don't view the deal as being incrementally positive enough to warrant the scale of re-rating the stock has seen," J.P. Morgan analyst Bill Peterson said. Rather, the government's stake can be viewed as a "backstop" for the company in an otherwise lackluster lithium market, he wrote.

J.P. Morgan has a $5 price target for the stock, which ended Thursday's session at $7.39.

Lithium Americas shares tumbled on the downgrade, with the stock falling 21.7% on Thursday, registering its biggest daily percentage decline since April 18, 2024, when it fell 29.71%, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Set against the backdrop of intensifying competition with China, the federal government recently also took a stake in rare-earths producer MP Materials Corp. (MP). However, there are big differences between the rare-earth and lithium industries, according to Peterson. "Rare earths have more explicit ties to defense and national security and are far less available outside of China's control, unlike lithium, which can be found in Australia and Latin America," he said.

But the analyst likes Lithium Americas' Thacker Pass mining project, a joint venture with a subsidiary of General Motors Co. (GM), and sees a pathway for it to be profitable once ramped up.

Lithium Americas describes Thacker Pass as the world's largest known measured lithium resource and reserve. Construction of the mine began in 2023, and "mechanical completion" is expected in 2027, Lithium Americas recently told MarketWatch. The company said it is on schedule to be in production in late 2027, and will scale up in 2028 to full production of 40,000 metric tons per year of battery-quality lithium carbonate.

Of 13 analysts surveyed by FactSet, two have a buy rating, seven have a hold rating and four have an underweight or sell rating for Lithium Americas.

Lithium Americas shares have climbed 148.8% in 2025 and MP Materials' stock is up 437.4%, compared with the S&P 500 index's SPX gain of 12.7%.

-James Rogers
